questions.question: Why does poor sleep make anxiety worse, according to the lesson?

options.text: Sleep deprivation causes the prefrontal cortex to overactivate, making overthinking worse
A lack of quality sleep leaves the amygdala on high alert, making you more susceptible to anxiety
Poor sleep causes the body to produce too much serotonin, which triggers anxiety
Sleep deprivation directly reduces the body's ability to produce adrenaline

questions.explanation: Sleep is when your brain cleans house — it processes emotions, consolidates memories, and resets your nervous system. Without it, your amygdala (the brain's alarm center) stays on high alert, lowering your anxiety threshold and making you more reactive to everyday stressors.

questions.question: What is the 'gut-brain axis' mentioned in the lesson?

options.text: A breathing technique that connects the belly and the mind
A powerful connection between gut health and brain chemistry and mood
The physical tension that builds in the stomach when you are anxious
A meditation practice focused on body scanning

questions.explanation: The gut-brain axis is a powerful biological connection between your digestive system and your brain. An unhealthy gut can send stress signals to the brain, directly affecting your mood and anxiety levels. This is why nourishing your body with whole foods and staying hydrated supports your mental health as well.

questions.question: How does movement help reduce anxiety?

options.text: It distracts your brain from anxious thoughts by making you focus on physical effort
It burns off excess adrenaline and cortisol and releases mood-enhancing endorphins
It increases heart rate, which desensitizes you to the physical symptoms of anxiety
It only helps when done at high intensity for at least one hour

questions.explanation: Movement is one of the most powerful, fast-acting anti-anxiety tools available. It burns off the excess stress hormones (adrenaline and cortisol) that fuel the anxiety response, releases tension from muscles, and triggers the release of endorphins that naturally elevate mood.

questions.question: What is the lesson's key message about approach to these three physical pillars?

options.text: You must master all three simultaneously before they have any effect
Intensity matters most — one big effort per week is more effective than small daily actions
Consistency over intensity — small daily actions build a more effective foundation than occasional intense efforts
Nutrition is the most important pillar and should be addressed before the others

questions.explanation: The lesson emphasizes consistency over intensity. A short walk every day is more beneficial for anxiety than one intense workout once a week. Small, sustainable improvements to your sleep, nutrition, and movement create a compounding physical foundation that supports all the other skills you have learned.

questions.question: Looking at the three pillars — sleep, nutrition, and movement — which one do you most need to strengthen right now? What is one small, specific action you can commit to this week to improve it?
questions.explanation: The most effective change is one that is specific and small enough that you cannot talk yourself out of it. Instead of 'sleep better,' try 'I will put my phone away 30 minutes before bed.' Instead of 'exercise more,' try 'I will take a 10-minute walk after lunch on Tuesday and Thursday.' Specific, achievable actions are the seeds of lasting habits.
